personSummary.value: Longtime vegetable gardener who feels useful when checking plants, carrying a small watering can, or confirming the garden gate. Short instructions work better than group prompts. Rainy evenings and blocked garden-view walks can increase pacing near exits. Morning medication is easier after he checks the balcony plants.

value.description: Rain and missed garden-view routines have recently led to pacing near windows or exits.
Balcony plant checks supported smoother morning medication acceptance.

citations.quote: let him check the small balcony plants first

value: Offer a supervised plant check or small watering task before breakfast care.
Move him to the lounge window when rain prevents the usual garden walk.
Use short instructions and avoid broad group prompts.

citations.quote: moved him to the lounge window and he settled

value: Rain blocking the usual garden view or walk.
Unanswered concern about whether the garden gate is locked.
Group activity prompts that do not give him a concrete task.

citations.quote: asked if the gate was locked
when the rain kept him indoors

value.approach: Show him the garden from the lounge window and verbally confirm the gate is secure.

citations.quote: showed him the garden from the lounge

value.approach: Let him check the balcony plants first, then give the next instruction briefly.

citations.quote: accepted after breakfast once staff let him check
inspect a plant or carry a small watering can

handoffBrief.value: Masao Nakamura calms with concrete gardening tasks and short instructions; if rain blocks the garden walk, use the lounge window and confirm the gate rather than debating the concern.

citations.quote: Staff moved him to the lounge window and he settled

value.claim: Watch for exit pacing on rainy evenings when the garden-view routine is interrupted.
